🟡 Burrenbeo Trust

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

The Burren Programme supports sustainable farming in western Ireland through its "pocket, head, and heart" approach– creating viable economic incentives for farmers ("pocket"), offering technical guidance and knowledge-sharing ("head"), and fostering trust and emotional connection with local communities ("heart").

Bioregional Context

Located in the west of Ireland along the Atlantic coast, the Burren is characterized by its unique limestone landscape. The region contains approximately 70% of Ireland's native flora, including a remarkable mix of Arctic, Alpine, and Mediterranean plants. This biodiverse landscape has been continuously farmed for approximately 6,000 years, creating a delicate balance between human activity and natural systems.

Place
Burren, Ennis, Ireland

Tags

🌾 Regenerative_Agriculture ✅ Result_Based_Mechanisms 📉 Ecological_Monitoring 🔀 Grants_Programs 👩‍🌾 Farmer_Support ⛓️ Value_Chain_Development 📷 Visitor_Experiences 🪢 Network_Weaving 💬 Peer_Learning 🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center 🎒 Learning_Programs 🎉 Congressing_and_Events

Background


The Burren is a bare karstified limestone landscape in the west of Ireland, on the Atlantic coast—a rare and unique place that contains about 70% of Ireland's native flora. The region is remarkable for its mix of Arctic, Alpine, and Mediterranean plants growing together on these grasslands. In a country facing a declared biodiversity crisis and climate crisis, the Burren represents one of the last great landscapes that is relatively intact and highly biodiverse.

Yet this landscape is fundamentally a farmed one—people have lived and farmed using livestock here for approximately 6,000 years, with archaeological sites dating to that period. The Burren has always existed in a delicate balance with farming: too much farming and the flowers start to disappear due to overly intensive management, but too little farming and the Burren tends to revert to scrub and woodland, losing much of its biodiversity as well.

Origins and Network


The mission of the Burren Programme has been to work with farmers, viewing them as a resource and mobilizing that resource to improve the ecosystem services they deliver from this landscape. This means moving from a position where farmers have just been rewarded and encouraged to produce food, to one where they produce food in a way that also produces these other increasingly rare and important services for society.

Philosophy & Approach


The Burren Programme's approach has crystallized around three fundamental principles that are essential for mobilizing farmers as an environmental resource: the pocket, the head, and the heart.

Activities

🔸

Results-Based Farming Program

 🔸

Farm Infrastructure Support Initiative

 🔸

New Revenue Development

 🔸

Burren Peer-to-Peer Learning Network

 🔸

Farming Hub

 🔸

Burren Public Engagement Program

Vision and Legacy


Fifteen years of field monitoring under the results-based model show year-on-year gains in environmental health since 2010. For under one million euros a year, the programme has bent the curve on biodiversity decline. Evaluations underline a wider point: when the relationship between people and place works, farming can deliver for nature, for rural economies, and for social outcomes together—not as trade-offs.
